text: The surgeon begins the dissection for a primary epigastric supraumbilical hernia repair, starting with the creation of a peritoneal flap several centimeters lateral to the median fat pad. The thin peritoneum is carefully manipulated to avoid tearing, with the surgeon using sharp dissection to separate adventitial fibers between the posterior sheath and the peritoneum.

The surgeon uses their left hand to push down on the median fat pad while dissecting across to the contralateral side, avoiding grabbing the peritoneum to prevent tearing. The peritoneal flap is opened for maximum exposure, and the pocket size is adjusted by extending the incision inferiorly. Blunt and sharp dissection is performed with the right hand above and below the hernia defect, revealing a small umbilical hernia that is reduced. The dissection continues until the median fat is lost on the contralateral side, and the hernia defect is reduced while keeping the peritoneum intact.

The surgeon performs blunt dissection with scissors to separate the thin peritoneum from the sticky posterior sheath, aiming to avoid creating holes. The dissection progresses laterally, resembling the technique used in transversus abdominis release (TAR), with gentle downward pressure revealing adventitial tissue. A pocket is created to accommodate a 15x15 Marlex mesh, ensuring redundancy for flap closure without tension.

The surgeon places a fifteen by fifteen Marlex mesh into the abdomen and uses a zero V-lock suture to close the defect in a transverse orientation. The umbilical pseudosec is imbricated to create an 'innie' appearance and prevent seroma formation. The insufflation pressure is reduced to eight for the closure process.

The surgeon places a 3-0 silk suture at the center of the mesh before inserting it into the abdomen, positioning it through the center of the hernia defect. The mesh is centered within the flap, ensuring coverage of approximately seven centimeters around the defect. The surgeon adjusts the mesh, pulling it up to the abdominal wall to prevent folding, and suspends large sacs to keep them elevated within the abdomen.
